# **Comparative Technical Audit and Synthesis of Electromyographic Silent Speech Interface Research**

The evolution of silent speech interfaces (SSIs) from specialized laboratory equipment to accessible consumer-grade hardware represents a transformative shift in neuroprosthetics and human-computer interaction. This transition is characterized by a move from 24-bit, high-density research systems to 12-bit, sparse-electrode configurations utilizing off-the-shelf components. However, the integrity of clinical and technical claims in this field relies heavily on the precise interpretation of foundational literature. A rigorous audit of current research reveals that many performance metrics and biophysical interpretations attributed to seminal works require correction or contextual refinement to ensure the validity of future cost-reduced implementations. The following analysis synthesizes the biophysical constraints, hardware limitations, and algorithmic frameworks of surface electromyography (sEMG) based speech recognition, while correcting historical inaccuracies in the citation of vocabulary sizes, accuracy rates, and physiological mechanisms.

## **Biophysical Mechanisms of Subvocal Phonation and Articulation**

Subvocalization involves the internal articulation of words through the activation of speech musculature without the expulsion of air or the vibration of vocal folds. This process generates surface electromyographic signals that typically reside in the ![][image1] range.1 These signals are the peripheral manifestations of motor commands originating in the primary motor cortex and traversing various cranial nerves. The coordination required for even a limited command vocabulary involves a sophisticated interplay between the trigeminal, facial, and laryngeal nerves.

The primary articulatory muscles utilized in silent speech tasks include the mentalis (chin), the anterior belly of the digastric, and the mylohyoid. The mentalis, innervated by the facial nerve (CN VII), is responsible for the elevation and protrusion of the lower lip, providing a superficial and high-amplitude signal source for sEMG.1 Conversely, the submental group, including the digastric and mylohyoid muscles, is innervated by the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve (CN V3) and controls jaw depression and tongue root position.1

Research into laryngeal activity indicates that laryngeal elevation and vocal fold tensioning are detectable even during purely silent speech tasks through electrodes placed over the thyrohyoid and sternohyoid muscles.1 These muscles are innervated by the first cervical nerve (C1) and the ansa cervicalis, respectively, providing a neural pathway distinct from the articulatory muscles.1 This neurological independence is a critical factor in SSI design, as it suggests that laryngeal sensors can provide orthogonal information regarding phonation intent, which can be used to distinguish speech activity from non-speech physiological noise.1

| Target Muscle | Innervation Pathway | Depth Category | Volume Conduction Constraint |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Mentalis | Facial Nerve (CN VII) | Superficial | Minimal attenuation; high signal clarity |
| Ant. Digastric | Trigeminal (CN V3) | Intermediate | Significant crosstalk with mylohyoid |
| Mylohyoid | Trigeminal (CN V3) | Intermediate | Subject to depth-related low-pass filtering |
| Genioglossus | Hypoglossal (CN XII) | Deep (![][image2]) | Signals often obscured at the surface |
| Thyrohyoid | Cervical (C1 via XII) | Intermediate | Captures gross laryngeal excursion |

Table 1: Biophysical and anatomical properties of orofacial muscle groups in sEMG research.1

The phenomenon of volume conduction represents a fundamental physical constraint on sEMG performance. As electrical potentials travel from the muscle fibers to the skin surface, the intervening tissue—comprised of resting muscle, subdermal fat, and skin—acts as a spatial low-pass filter.1 The physical distance, often characterized as being in the ![][image2] range for deep lingual muscles such as the genioglossus, attenuates the high-frequency components of motor unit action potentials (MUAPs).1 Because discriminative MUAPs for subtle speech movements reside primarily in the ![][image3] frequency range, surface electrodes frequently capture only macroscopic, gross motor recruitment patterns rather than the fine-grained phonetic detail necessary for large-vocabulary recognition.1

## **Technical Discrepancies in Seminal SSI Literature**

A critical component of this citation audit involves the reconciliation of performance metrics reported for the AlterEgo system and early NASA Ames research. Misinterpretations of these benchmarks have led to a conflated understanding of what constitutes state-of-the-art performance in varying hardware regimes.

### **Audit of the AlterEgo Benchmarks**

The AlterEgo system is widely cited for its high accuracy in silent speech tasks. However, the specific vocabulary size associated with its peak accuracy requires clarification. While the system utilized a cumulative vocabulary of approximately 20 words across diverse applications such as navigation and IoT control, the frequently cited ![][image4] accuracy rate refers specifically to a 10-digit vocabulary (0-9).1 Conflating this figure with a 20-word or 100-word set creates an inaccurate baseline for system comparisons. The system's hardware configuration—a 7-channel, 24-bit research-grade setup costing in excess of ![][image5]—utilized gold-plated electrodes and an ADS1299 ADC sampling at ![][image6].1 This high-resolution front-end allowed the system to achieve a noise floor of approximately ![][image7], a capability that is not replicable on 12-bit consumer hardware.1

### **Clarification of the Jou et al. NASA Research**

Historical citations often reference Jou et al. (2006) for a ![][image8] accuracy claim on subvocal EMG. The audit indicates a mismatch in these citations; the ![][image9] mean accuracy figure is actually associated with the 2007 ICASSP publication and subsequent NASA reports, rather than the 2006 Interspeech paper.1 Furthermore, the ![][image8] accuracy was achieved on a "mouthed" speech mode, where the articulators moved normally but no sound was produced.5 Jou's research actually documented an inability to conduct recognition on purely "mentally rehearsed" or "inner" speech due to the lack of detectable sEMG activity in those modes.5 This distinction is vital for understanding the difference between silent speech (motor execution) and imagined speech (purely cognitive).

### **The Laryngectomy Context in Meltzner 2017**

The work of Meltzner et al. (2017) is frequently cited in the context of healthy-subject silent speech interfaces. However, the audit reveals that the reported word error rate (WER) of ![][image10] on a 100-word vocabulary was achieved in a study specifically involving laryngectomy patients.1 These individuals, having had their larynx removed, often utilize "voiced whisper" or exaggerated articulation to communicate, which provides a significantly more robust sEMG signal than the "covert speech" attempted by healthy participants in privacy-focused SSI applications.8 The high-density sEMG arrays (8-16 electrodes) used in this research further distinguish its results from the sparse configurations typical of low-cost wearable devices.1

## **Comparison of Research and Consumer Hardware Specifications**

The divergence between high-end research systems and cost-reduced consumer systems is driven primarily by the resolution and noise characteristics of the analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) and the filtering characteristics of the analog front-end (AFE).

| Specification | Research Standard (ADS1299) | Consumer Grade (ESP32/AD8232) | Performance Impact |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Resolution | 24-bit | 12-bit | 4096:1 reduction in dynamic range 1 |
| Effective Resolution | \~24 bits | 9-10 bits | Limited by ±6 LSB integral nonlinearity 1 |
| Noise Floor | ![][image7] | ![][image11] | Obscures ![][image1] subvocal signals 1 |
| Bandpass Filter | ![][image12] (Variable) | ![][image13] | Attenuates discriminative MUAP frequencies 1 |
| Signal Gain | ![][image14] | ![][image15] | Higher gain scales noise and leads to saturation 1 |
| Total Cost | ![][image16] | ![][image17] | Democratization vs. Signal Integrity 1 |

Table 2: Technical hardware comparison between research-grade and consumer-grade SSI components.1

The AD8232 module, a common choice for ![][image18] systems, presents a specific challenge due to its built-in bandpass filter. Designed for cardiac monitoring, its ![][image13] passband is engineered to isolate the QRS complex of a heartbeat.1 In sEMG tasks, this filter eliminates the high-frequency articulatory detail that resides between ![][image19] and ![][image20].1 Consequently, systems utilizing this hardware are restricted to an "onset-only" detection paradigm, where the model classifies words based on the high-amplitude, transient neuromuscular initiation burst (\~80 ms) rather than continuous articulatory trajectories.1

## **Mathematical Modeling of the SSI Pipeline**

The extraction of features from low-resolution sEMG data requires robust mathematical frameworks that emphasize spectral shape over absolute amplitude. The utilization of Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs) in this domain is a direct adaptation from acoustic speech processing.

### **Feature Extraction and Mel-Scaling**

The sEMG signal ![][image21] is first decomposed into the frequency domain. Given the low sampling rates typical of consumer hardware (![][image6]), the Nyquist frequency is limited to ![][image22]. The power spectrum is calculated through the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) of windowed segments:

![][image23]  
where ![][image24] represents a Hamming or Hanning window used to mitigate spectral leakage.1 The mapping of these frequencies to the Mel-scale, though essentially linear at such low sampling rates, preserves the amplitude-invariance properties identified as essential for cross-intensity generalization.1 The log-filterbank energies are then decorrelated via the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) to produce the final coefficients:

![][image25]  
In a two-channel system, this results in a feature tensor ![][image26], where ![][image27] represents the number of time frames.1

### **1D Convolutional Neural Network Dynamics**

The classification of these feature tensors is most effectively handled by 1D Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). Unlike Transformers, which require large datasets to learn global relationships, CNNs possess a strong inductive bias that emphasizes local temporal patterns, matching the physics of phonemic transitions in sEMG.1 The convolutional operation for a single filter is defined as:

![][image28]  
where ![][image29] is the input MFCC sequence and ![][image30] is the kernel.1 By stacking these layers, the model learns to isolate the ![][image31] onset burst from the surrounding quantization noise.

| Model Architecture | Observed Accuracy (2-Ch) | Generalized Ceiling | Limitation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 1D CNN | ![][image32] | ![][image33] (Multi-Sess) | Limited by hardware SNR 1 |
| Transformer | ![][image34] | High Variance | Data-hungry; unstable on biodata 1 |
| LSTM | ![][image35] | Chance Baseline | No temporal structure beyond onset burst 1 |

Table 3: Performance of various machine learning architectures on 12-bit sEMG data.1

## **Algorithmic Interventions for Signal Degradation**

The most significant hurdle in deploying wearable SSIs is the dramatic performance drop caused by electrode displacement. A ![][image36] shift in electrode placement, common when a user reapplies the device, can degrade classification accuracy to near-chance levels (![][image37]).1 To counteract this, several software-based interventions are required.

### **Curriculum Learning and Signal Regimes**

Curriculum learning involves training a model on a spectrum of signal intensities to bridge the gap between loud vocalization and silent thought. This approach utilizes a multi-phase protocol:

1. **Overt Phase:** High-amplitude signals (![][image38]) provide clear phonetic boundaries.  
2. **Whispered/Mouthing Phases:** Intermediate amplitudes (![][image39]) introduce natural articulatory noise.  
3. **Exaggerated Phase:** Closed-mouth, high-tension signals (![][image40]) act as a critical bridge.  
4. **Covert Phase:** Minimal amplitude signals (![][image1]) often fall below the 12-bit noise floor.1

Training on the "Exaggerated" phase is particularly vital. Without it, the amplitude gap between mouthing and covert speech is often too large for the CNN to learn a shared geometric structure, resulting in a failure to generalize to the ![][image1] regime.1

### **Multi-Session Manifold Alignment**

The single most effective intervention for improving generalization on consumer sEMG is multi-session training with deliberate electrode repositioning. By exposing the model to the variability inherent in donning and doffing, it learns position-invariant onset features.1 Research indicates that while single-session accuracy might reach ![][image41], multi-session recovery can improve this figure to ![][image42] through the identification of stable spectral shapes within the neuromuscular initiation burst.1

### **Confidence Gating and UX Optimization**

To make an SSI practically usable, a "reject option" or confidence gating mechanism is necessary. A softmax probability threshold ![][image43] is applied to the classifier's output. A command is only executed if:

![][image44]  
At a threshold of ![][image45], systems have demonstrated an accuracy of ![][image46] on ![][image47] of accepted predictions.1 While this reduces the error rate, it introduces a ![][image48] rejection rate, creating a cognitive load that must be balanced with haptic or visual feedback to maintain user trust in the collaborative loop.1

## **The Necessity of Anatomical Orthogonality**

A central debate in SSI design is the comparative value of lingual-mandibular (chin \+ under-chin) and lingual-laryngeal (chin \+ throat) electrode configurations. Audit of recent controlled comparisons (Study A vs. Study B) provides a clear answer regarding their operational viability.

While both configurations achieve comparable single-session accuracies of approximately ![][image49], they capture fundamentally incompatible feature spaces.1 Cross-study transfer—training on one configuration and testing on the other—yields accuracies only slightly above chance (![][image50]).1 This suggests that the two configurations do not provide different views of the same signal but rather capture distinct physiological phenomena.

The throat sensor is deemed superior for multi-session stability because the larynx is neurologically independent of the jaw.1 The chin and under-chin sensors frequently exhibit significant inter-channel crosstalk, as they both encode "jaw moved" but fail to provide the dimensional relief needed to separate lateral tongue movements like "left" and "right".1 The throat sensor, capturing laryngeal elevation and vocal fold tension, provides a second independent axis of discrimination that breaks this signal crosstalk problem.1

| Configuration | Primary Muscle Pools | Neural Pathway | Stability Argument |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Chin \+ Under-Chin | Mentalis/Digastric | CN VII & CN V3 | Anatomically intrusive; high crosstalk 1 |
| Chin \+ Throat | Mentalis/Thyrohyoid | CN VII & C1 (Ansa) | Orthogonal; superior multi-session recovery 1 |

Table 4: Anatomical comparison of electrode placement strategies.1

## **Synthesis of Internal Speech Research: The Nieto Study**

The interpretation of the "Thinking Out Loud" dataset (Nieto et al., 2022\) is often cited to support the idea that silent speech is a direct proxy for inner speech. However, the audit clarifies that Nieto's work suggests inner speech involves motor plan inhibition.1 This has profound implications for BCI researchers: if the brain active inhibits the motor plan during "pure" thought, then peripheral sEMG systems will never be able to capture "imagined" speech that doesn't involve some residual neuromuscular recruitment.11

This finding validates the "Palm Pilot" or user-centric co-learning strategy utilized in low-cost SSI research. Rather than attempting to passively decode stray thoughts, successful systems teach the user to produce consistent, exaggerated motor strategies for each command.1 This shifts the burden of communication from the classifier to a collaborative human-machine loop, where the user adapts their subvocalization patterns to the system's hardware limitations.1

## **Social and Design Implications for Wearable SSIs**

The technical necessity of throat electrodes has direct implications for the form factor of future wearable devices. While submental (under-chin) placement is arguably more discrete, the multi-session stability of the throat position suggests that neck-based form factors—such as smart collars, necklaces, or chokers—are more viable for long-term use.1 Adhesive facial electrodes remain socially stigmatizing and irritating to the skin, further reinforcing the need for integrated wearable solutions that prioritize wearability over absolute articulatory resolution.1

Furthermore, the reporting of negative results in this field is critical. As Fanelli (2012) and Lotte et al. (2020) have noted, the decrease in published negative results artificially inflates the perceived robustness of BCI methods.1 The documentation of failures in chin-only configurations on 12-bit hardware serves to prevent future researchers from toiling in vain on uninformative sensor configurations.1

## **Conclusions and Future Trajectories**

The citation audit and technical analysis of electromyographic silent speech interfaces demonstrate that the "92% accuracy" benchmark often cited in literature must be strictly qualified by vocabulary size (10 digits) and hardware resolution (24-bit). For the emerging class of ![][image18] consumer-grade systems, a more realistic generalization ceiling resides at ![][image33] accuracy for a 6-class command set, provided that multi-session training and confidence gating are implemented.

The biophysical ceiling of sEMG-based SSIs is dictated by the depth of intrinsic lingual muscles and the spatial low-pass filtering of the human body. However, the hardware bottleneck of 12-bit quantization can be navigated through the intelligent application of 1D CNNs that prioritize neuromuscular onset bursts. The incompatible feature spaces between submental and laryngeal placements suggest that future high-performance systems should move toward 3-channel integration ![][image51] to achieve the dimensionality required for complex command sets.

Ultimately, the accessibility of silent speech technology depends on a "hardware-aware" software paradigm. By acknowledging the onset-only detection regime and the necessity of anatomical orthogonality, researchers can develop low-cost interfaces that provide meaningful communication aids to individuals with speech impairments or users in sensitive privacy environments. The move toward open-source replication packages and the rigorous audit of foundational claims ensure that the democratization of neuroprosthetics remains grounded in physical and neurological reality.

#### **Works cited**

1. main1.pdf  
2. Contributions of Individual Muscles to the Submental Surface Electromyogram During Swallowing \- PubMed, accessed March 1, 2026, [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10599620/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10599620/)

5. Speech recognition for vocalized and subvocal modes of production using surface EMG signals from the neck and face \- ResearchGate, accessed March 1, 2026, [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/221478374\_Speech\_recognition\_for\_vocalized\_and\_subvocal\_modes\_of\_production\_using\_surface\_EMG\_signals\_from\_the\_neck\_and\_face](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/221478374_Speech_recognition_for_vocalized_and_subvocal_modes_of_production_using_surface_EMG_signals_from_the_neck_and_face)

7. Silent Speech Recognition as an Alternative Communication Device for Persons with Laryngectomy \- PubMed, accessed March 1, 2026, [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29552581/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29552581/)  
8. Silent Speech Recognition as an Alternative Communication Device for Persons with Laryngectomy \- PMC, accessed March 1, 2026, [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5851476/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5851476/)  
9. Future Solutions for Voice Rehabilitation in Laryngectomees: A Review of Technologies Based on Electrophysiological Signals \- PMC, accessed March 1, 2026, [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895460/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9895460/)  
10. Ensemble Machine Learning Model for Inner Speech Recognition: A Subject- Specific Investigation \- arXiv.org, accessed March 1, 2026, [https://arxiv.org/pdf/2412.17824](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2412.17824)  
11. From pronounced to imagined: improving speech decoding with multi-condition EEG data \- PMC, accessed March 1, 2026, [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12245923/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12245923/)
